title: Large Fluctuations in Open Quantum Systems
authors: V. Yu. Mylnikov, S. O. Potashin, A. Kamenev
abstract: We study statistics of atypical measurement outcomes in the steady states of driven open quantum systems. In equilibrium, the probability distribution over the phase space, as encoded in, e.g., the Wigner function, is analytic in the phase-space coordinates. We show that this property is generically lost in driven dissipative systems: their {\it large-deviation function} develops lines and surfaces across which its derivatives are discontinuous. As an illustrative example, we consider a parametrically driven Kerr oscillator coupled linearly and/or nonlinearly to a dissipative bath. Rare fluctuations in the amplitude and phase of the induced oscillations are governed by semiclassical instanton trajectories of the corresponding Keldysh-Lindblad action. We demonstrate that a given fluctuation can be realized through multiple distinct instanton trajectories. The competition between these trajectories leads to abrupt switching of the dominant instanton and, consequently, to non-analytic features in the large-deviation function.
summary: This paper investigates rare fluctuations in driven open quantum systems using advanced semiclassical methods. It demonstrates that unlike equilibrium states, these non-equilibrium steady states exhibit large-deviation functions with sharp, non-analytic features. These features arise fundamentally from the competition between multiple distinct instanton trajectories governing the system's dynamics.
main_problem: The study focuses on characterizing atypical measurement outcomes (rare fluctuations) in the steady states of driven, open quantum systems.
main_result: The large-deviation function for these systems is generically non-analytic due to the competition between multiple competing semiclassical instanton trajectories. This contrasts with equilibrium systems where analyticity holds.
method: The analysis employs semiclassical methods derived from Keldysh path integrals and WKB approximations, relating rare events to instanton solutions of the effective action.
model_or_system: The model is an open quantum system, exemplified by a parametrically driven Kerr oscillator coupled to a dissipative bath, governed by a Lindblad master equation.
key_observables: Large-deviation function $S(x, p)$ and the phase-slip rate $\Gamma$.
important_parameters: Drive strength ($G$), detuning ($\Delta$), dissipation rates ($\kappa, \eta, \kappa_\phi$), and the semiclassical parameter $\hbar$ (or $\delta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the WKB approximation for the path integral and identifying an invariant subspace parameterized by $(\alpha(t), \chi(t))$ in the Keldysh formalism.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate real-time instanton trajectories spanning a fluctuation Riemann surface, showing multiple paths to endpoints. They also map out Stokes lines and anti-Stokes lines on phase space plots, visualizing where non-analyticities occur.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the general problem of large deviations in open systems to applying it to a specific driven oscillator model. It develops the semiclassical formalism using Keldysh theory, identifies the multi-valued nature of solutions (Riemann surface), and finally analyzes how Stokes phenomena dictate non-analytic features in the resulting large-deviation function.
why_interesting: This work provides deep insights into non-equilibrium statistical mechanics by showing that quantum dissipation can induce complex, non-analytic structures in fluctuation statistics, extending concepts from classical instanton theory to the quantum domain.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a,b) Stationary Wigner function W0(α, α∗) and effective potential −ln W0(α, α∗) (c,d) vs. phase-space coordinates Re(α) and Im(α). The first row presents the case of zero dephasing (κϕ = 0), where the exact (a,c) and WKB (d) solutions present (see Eqs. (15)-(17) and Appendix A). The second row shows the nonzero dephasing case (κϕ = 0.2), which does not admit the holomorphic decomposition (15) and is calculated numerically. The system parameters are set to G = 5, ∆= 2, κ = 0.4, η = 1, U = 0.1. The red dots denote locations of the classical fixed point (10)-(11).

Figure 2. The real-time instanton trajectories, Eq. 37, spanning the fluctuation Riemann surface, A(α, β) = 0. The instanton paths start at classical stable points (red dots). (a-c) The subcritical drive, G < | ˜∆|; (d-e) – the bistable regime, G > | ˜∆|. (a), (d) Trajectories projections on the β-plane; for a given end-point β there is a unique trajectory, leading to it. (b,c) and (e,f) Trajectories projections on the two sheets of the α-plane. There are two trajectories leading to each end-point α: they both start on the minus-sheet (b), (e), one (orange) stays on this sheet, while the second (green) hits the cut (blue dashed line) and emerges on the second (plus) sheet (c), (f). The...

Figure 3. Stokes lines (red) on the complex α plane, as de- fined by Eq. (47). Within the inner Stokes region (blue), only the instanton with S−action contributes to the Wigner func- tion. Within the outer Stokes region (green), both instantons with S± contribute. The two actions equalize along the anti- Stokes lines (black), given by Eq. (23). The large deviation function is non-analytic along these lines. One possible choice of branch cuts of the function ϕ(±) 0 (α/αb) are depicted as blue dashed lines. The branch cut of the logarithm ln(α2) in S+ should be chosen within the inner Stokes region. The system parameters are set to G = 2, ∆= 2, κ = 0.4, η = 1, U = 0.1, κϕ = 0.

Figure 4. (a,b) The large-deviation function from the in- stanton approach (49) (red) and the exact solution (15) – (18) (blue) vs. the angle φ = arg(α/αb) for a fixed ra- dius |α| = 3.5|αb|. The system parameters are set to G = 5, ∆= 2, κ = 0.4, η = 1, U = 0.1, κϕ = 0.

Figure 5. The α-plane flow and the phase-slip instanton path (orange line) in the bistable regime. The phase-slip path starts at the classical fixed point α = α0 and ends at the saddle point α = 0 (red dots). From the saddle point to the opposite stationary point, the system evolves classically (i.e., in χ = 0 subspace), according to Eq. (9), with no action ac- cumulated along this part of the phase-slip path. The system parameters are set to G = 5, ∆= 2, κ = 0.4, η = 1, U = 0.1, κϕ = 0.
